title: The Pie

jokes.text: Aren't mannequins kind of an insult to your imagination? You couldn't visualize a sweater, so we'll show you on this life-size, snotty puppet.
jokes.setup: Jerry opens with the question of what mannequins are fundamentally for — displaying clothes.
jokes.punchline: 'life-size, snotty puppet' reframes mannequins as condescending objects, insulting the shopper's intelligence.
jokes.explanation: Strong Seinfeld-style observational premise. 'Snotty puppet' is a fresh, evocative phrase. Sets up the episode's mannequin theme in stand-up form. Solid craft but not transcendent.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What is the purpose of a mannequin anyway? I mean, they're just standing there. They don't do anything. They don't talk, they don't move... they're like a snotty puppet.

jokes.text: When they're finished they become crash-test dummies. The end of the line for a mannequin. 'Whatever happened to Bob?' 'Have you seen that new Volvo commercial? He's got a bull's-eye right in his face.'
jokes.setup: Mannequins are used for fashion, then discarded.
jokes.punchline: They become crash-test dummies — imagining them as characters with names and tragic fates, the 'bull's-eye right in his face' being the morbid visual payoff.
jokes.explanation: Exceptionally original bit — personifying mannequins with a career arc ending in vehicular destruction is a dark, clever extension of the premise. The Volvo name-drop with the specific visual detail 'bull's-eye right in his face' elevates it beyond the setup. Among the best stand-up bits in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So mannequins, they start out in fashion. They're on top of the world, right? Modeling clothes, looking fabulous. Then what happens? They end up in a car, getting smashed into a wall at 40 miles per hour.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's the mannequin career arc. One day you're strutting down the runway in Milan, the next day you're 'Crash Test Dummy Number 7.'

jokes.text: Mannequins are only used for car accidents and fashion — two situations that it's impossible for us to imagine ourselves: well-dressed or getting killed.
jokes.setup: Mannequins serve two purposes: fashion and crash testing.
jokes.punchline: The two things humans can't imagine for themselves are being well-dressed OR getting killed — the juxtaposition is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The structural payoff here is excellent — the parallel between 'well-dressed' and 'getting killed' as equally unimaginable human experiences is a genuinely clever inversion. Clean, economical, and lands with a satisfying snap.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm not fashionable enough to use a mannequin as a fashion reference, and I'm not morbid enough to use one to imagine my own death.

jokes.text: 'Hey, they're not mannequins. They're the life-deprived.'
jokes.setup: Jerry imagines a pro-mannequin advocacy group offended by the term 'mannequin.'
jokes.punchline: The politically correct euphemism 'life-deprived' for an inanimate object mocks sensitivity culture.
jokes.explanation: Solid Seinfeld move — applying PC-language logic to an absurd subject. 'Life-deprived' is a memorable coinage. Tight and punchy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what they should call mannequins? Life-deprived. That's the PC term. 'Oh, we need a life-deprived person over here for the window display.' Instead of saying the mannequin is dead.

jokes.text: Jerry repeatedly offers Audrey apple pie; she refuses without explanation, just shaking her head each time.
jokes.setup: Jerry is eating what he calls the best apple pie in the city and naturally offers some to his date.
jokes.punchline: Audrey refuses repeatedly without any explanation, creating escalating social discomfort — the refusal itself becomes absurdist.
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is structural — the repetition of 'No' to increasingly earnest offers becomes absurdist through escalation. This scene plants the episode's central mystery. The silence IS the joke. Gains rewatch value once you know Poppie's secret.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: She won't even taste it. I'm like, 'Just taste it!' And she does this thing... (shakes head) No. Just... no. Like I'm offering her a bowl of dirt.

jokes.text: Jerry reenacts Audrey's silent head-shake to George and Elaine at Monk's.
jokes.setup: Jerry is recounting Audrey's refusal to taste the pie.
jokes.punchline: Jerry physically demonstrates the silent head-shake, turning a verbal recap into a visual performance.
jokes.explanation: A competent physical callback that gets a laugh from the absurdity of the situation. The visual re-enactment sells the weirdness better than dialogue alone could.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: She's not diabetic. She just carries donuts in her purse.

jokes.text: She just kept shaking her head like this: [demonstrates exaggerated head shake]
jokes.setup: Jerry recounts to George that Audrey refused the pie without explanation
jokes.punchline: Jerry physically demonstrates Audrey's silent headshake, making it visual and comedic in repetition
jokes.explanation: A functional physical callback. Jerry reenacting the headshake amplifies the absurdity and helps sell the premise to George. Not a standout joke but performs its narrative function with some physical comedy value.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why would she be offended? I suggested the moving walkway!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: The moving walkway? That's the most inoffensive thing you could possibly suggest.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Exactly! It's just a practical solution. She gets tired, boom, moving walkway.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's not like you told her to take the stairs or something. The moving walkway is for everyone.

jokes.text: Jerry tells the group he told Audrey 'we should have moving walkways all over the city,' and George, Elaine, and Jerry all agree it's a great idea with mounting enthusiasm.
jokes.setup: George asks if Jerry said something that might have offended Audrey.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's completely sincere and mundane suggestion about walkways is validated by everyone at the table as obviously brilliant, derailing the conversation entirely.
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld group dynamic — the entire gang agrees on an utterly trivial thing with total conviction while the actual serious topic (Audrey's strange behavior) is completely abandoned. 'They never try. What's the harm? No harm!' is a perfectly calibrated escalation of shared absurdist logic. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I've never broken up with someone over pie.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, I once broke up with a guy because he wouldn't share his pie with me.

jokes.text: Elaine: 'I once broke up with someone for not offering me pie.'
jokes.setup: George says he's never broken up with anyone for not tasting pie.
jokes.punchline: Elaine one-ups the absurdity — she broke up with someone for not OFFERING her pie, a subtle but somehow more petty offense.
jokes.explanation: This is the best single line in the episode. The distinction between 'not tasting pie' and 'not offering pie' is hilariously specific and escalates the absurdity perfectly. It's entirely Elaine — demanding, food-focused, with iron-clad social etiquette on trivial matters. The follow-up ('he could be eating a hero, he wouldn't offer me anything — it's a sickness') closes it perfectly. Justifies the 8+ score.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He's sick. He has a sickness. It's about the food. He won't share his food. I mean, who does that? Who doesn't share their food?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: It's a sickness. That's what it is. A sickness.

jokes.text: Elaine: 'He could be eating a hero — he wouldn't offer me anything. It's a sickness.'
jokes.setup: Elaine explains her breakup rationale from the previous line.
jokes.punchline: 'It's a sickness' — diagnosing someone's failure to offer food as a clinical disorder.
jokes.explanation: The capper to the previous line. 'It's a sickness' is the perfect Elaine justification — she delivers self-righteous pronouncements with complete sincerity. The specificity of 'a hero' grounds the absurdity. Works as a callback and a standalone.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I've never heard of a woman getting a pebble in her shoe.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What?! What are you talking about? Women get pebbles in their shoes all the time!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Why would that be a man thing?

jokes.text: Jerry: 'Well, I can't walk anywhere now. I'm just gonna be wishing there were walkways.'
jokes.setup: The walkway conversation has concluded, but it planted a seed.
jokes.punchline: Jerry is now ruined for regular walking — the trivial idea has genuinely altered his perspective.
jokes.explanation: Nice extension of the walkway bit — the idea that discussing a utopian convenience has made normal life worse. Very Seinfeldian.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: They take a real woman, they paint her white, they put her in a window.

jokes.text: Kramer bursts in with a pebble in his shoe, says 'I never heard of that happening to a woman.'
jokes.setup: Kramer enters with a limp from a pebble in his shoe — a mundane complaint.
jokes.punchline: He's surprised a woman got a pebble in her shoe, as if it's a gender-exclusive affliction.
jokes.explanation: Mild Kramer eccentricity. The logic is absurd enough to get a laugh but it's a minor moment, more of a transition gag.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: These companies, they don't know what they're doing. I mean, look at this thing. It's got no life, no character. You gotta have a certain... je ne sais quoi.

jokes.text: Kramer describes the mannequin: 'It's like they chopped off your arms and legs, dipped you in plastic, screwed you all back together, and stuck you on a pedestal. It's really quite exquisite.'
jokes.setup: Kramer is trying to explain the uncanny resemblance between Elaine and the mannequin.
jokes.punchline: 'It's really quite exquisite' — after a horrifying description of dismemberment and plastic-dipping, Kramer signs off with an aesthetic compliment delivered with total sincerity.
jokes.explanation: This is among the best Kramer lines in the episode. The escalating horror of the description ('chopped off your arms... dipped you in plastic... screwed you all back together') is pure Kramer — he's so focused on communicating the visual similarity that he's oblivious to how grotesque the description sounds. 'It's really quite exquisite' as the capstone is perfectly placed. High character integration because only Kramer could say this with such sincere admiration. Justifies the 8.5+ score.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm living in my parents' house, I'm thirty-six years old, I live with my mother and father, I sleep in a single bed with cartoon characters on my bedspread, my bureau is filled with - with toy figurines still in their original packaging.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: When I go out, I have to tell my mother where I'm going, I have to be back by a certain time. It's like a curfew! I'm not a child!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: And the itch... the itch is always there.

jokes.text: Elaine's reaction beat to Kramer's 'exquisite' compliment — visible discomfort/flattery conflict as she processes being compared to a plastic puppet.
jokes.setup: Kramer has just described dismembering and plastifying Elaine as 'exquisite.'
jokes.punchline: Elaine's conflict between horror and a flicker of being flattered by the comparison to a $1200 dress-wearing mannequin.
jokes.explanation: A beat moment that plays off Kramer's previous line. Julia Louis-Dreyfus's reaction would sell it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's like Invasion of the Body Snatchers. They replaced her with a mannequin and she's still the same person!

jokes.text: Kramer explains his back itch: 'It's this itch. I was watching TV without my shirt on, and my couch cushion didn't have any fabric on it.'
jokes.setup: Kramer apparently needs his back scratched
jokes.punchline: His explanation — shirtless TV watching on a bare couch cushion — is so specifically Kramer-ish that the image itself is the joke
jokes.explanation: Character comedy. The specific image of Kramer sitting shirtless on a fabric-less couch cushion is funny because it's so perfectly him — chaotic, unselfconscious, slightly feral. Sets up the back-scratching subplot.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You can't fall asleep! That's how they get you. That's how the pod people work. You fall asleep, they replace you with a replica.

jokes.text: Kramer says the mannequin is at a store called 'Rinitze' — George responds 'Real boss.'
jokes.setup: The gang is discussing the mannequin store.
jokes.punchline: 'Real boss' — George's dated, enthusiastic slang for the cool suits in the store.
jokes.explanation: Minor character moment. 'Real boss' as enthusiastic George-speak is mildly amusing but not a crafted joke.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, maybe *I* should be rolling my eyes at *you*. Did you ever think about that? You work in a store all day, you see mannequins, you know what they look like. I'm just trying to buy a blouse here.

jokes.text: George announces he needs a new suit because he has a 'second interview with MacKenzie' and they're 'taking him to lunch on Friday.'
jokes.setup: George pivots to his own agenda — the suit trip is now justified by his job interview.
jokes.punchline: The mundane announcement plants the seed for the suit subplot — delivered with George's characteristic self-importance.
jokes.explanation: Plot setup more than a joke, but delivered with George's self-important fussiness. Gets rewatch credit as setup for the payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Clerk
dialogue.line: I don't think this dress is right for you.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What do you mean? I'm not worthy of this dress? This dress is a rag! It's a potato sack with sleeves!

jokes.text: George offers the scratch rod to Jerry; Jerry says 'Keep it' without even thinking about it.
jokes.setup: George has been using some implement for his back itch.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's instant, breezy dismissal — zero hesitation about not wanting Kramer's back-scratch tool.
jokes.explanation: A quick, clean beat. Jerry's economy of revulsion is funny but minor.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: These are Eurotrash rags! Designer clothes that cost a fortune and they fall apart like they're made of tissue paper!

jokes.text: Jerry stares at the mannequin and says to George: 'It's like some pod landed from another planet and took your body.'
jokes.setup: They've arrived at the store and are staring at the mannequin that looks like Elaine.
jokes.punchline: The sci-fi body-snatcher framing for a retail mannequin is a delightfully overwrought comparison.
jokes.explanation: A solid, playful line. The body-snatcher metaphor escalates the strangeness of the mannequin subplot nicely.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Oh, fairness. That's what it's about. You know, it's not fair that some people are taller than other people. That's not fair. The taller people, they have an advantage. Do you know how hard it is to be short? Especially when you're trying to buy a suit.

jokes.text: Jerry tells Elaine 'Don't fall asleep' — a reference to the body-snatcher pod narrative.
jokes.setup: Jerry has framed the mannequin situation as a body-snatching event.
jokes.punchline: 'Don't fall asleep' — in Invasion of the Body Snatchers lore, you're taken when you sleep.
jokes.explanation: Tight callback in three words. Works on the sci-fi reference level and as a silly warning to Elaine. Economy is near-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're not a psycho.

dialogue.speaker: Woman
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Unlike Audrey.

jokes.text: The store salesperson tells George 'You are perfect for that suit' — immediately after George spots it.
jokes.setup: George is browsing suits at Rinitze, excited about a specific one.
jokes.punchline: The salesperson's flattery is transparently self-serving, but George is completely susceptible to it.
jokes.explanation: Setup for the suit subplot. The flattery is obvious but George's gullibility to it is in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So I'm investigating this woman, right? I'm following her, checking her out, seeing what she's all about.

dialogue.speaker: Woman
dialogue.line: That's not investigating. That's stalking. You're a psycho.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm a psycho? You're the one who noticed I was following you!

jokes.text: Elaine confronts the snooty saleswoman about the mannequin; the saleswoman rolls her eyes at her complaint. Jerry: 'Did you just roll your eyes at him?' The saleswoman: 'If anybody should be rolling their eyes, it is me at him about you.'
jokes.setup: Elaine has challenged the saleswoman about the mannequin's resemblance to her.
jokes.punchline: The saleswoman's retort is a labyrinthine social put-down — she's not just dismissing Elaine, she's establishing a full hierarchy of eye-rolling rights.
jokes.explanation: Sharp antagonist line. The recursiveness of 'me at him about you' creates a satisfying verbal tangle. The saleswoman's haughtiness is established economically.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Elaine, would you scratch my back?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Kramer, what is this?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: It's a funky adventure.

jokes.text: Saleswoman: 'I think maybe you're flattering yourself. That mannequin is wearing a $1200 Gaultier dress.'
jokes.setup: Elaine insists the mannequin looks like her.
jokes.punchline: The saleswoman implies the mannequin is too well-dressed to be associated with Elaine — the insult is delivered through the dress's price tag.
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed put-down. Using the dress's price to establish the mannequin's superiority to Elaine is elegantly condescending.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Can you scratch my back?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I have a strict no-scratch-my-back policy.

jokes.text: Elaine: 'What are you saying, that I'm not good enough for this hideous dress?'
jokes.setup: The saleswoman has implied the $1200 Gaultier dress is beyond Elaine.
jokes.punchline: Elaine accepts the 'good enough' framing but pivots to calling it 'hideous' — she's simultaneously offended at being found unworthy of something she doesn't even like.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Elaine line. The self-contradiction — fighting for acceptance into a club she'd publicly reject — is perfectly human and very specifically Elaine. 'This hideous dress' lands perfectly. Justifies the 8+ score: the joke requires understanding Elaine's wounded pride and social competitiveness simultaneously.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: My back is killing me. I need something to scratch it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Use a back scratcher.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I don't have a back scratcher. I'm gonna spatula myself.

jokes.text: Elaine: 'Listen, Natasha... I wouldn't be caught dead wearing your crummy little Eurotrash rags.'
jokes.setup: The argument with the saleswoman is escalating.
jokes.punchline: Elaine storms into full insult mode with 'Eurotrash rags,' having just been wounded by the implication she wasn't good enough for those same clothes.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation. 'Eurotrash rags' is a sharp, specific insult. The callback from 'hideous dress' to 'Eurotrash rags' shows consistent character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I would be most grateful for your assistance in this matter of considerable dermal distress.

jokes.text: George looks at the price tag and says 'Party's over.'
jokes.setup: George has been trying on the suit with great enthusiasm.
jokes.punchline: The price tag instantly deflates his excitement — 'Party's over' is the perfect flat George response.
jokes.explanation: Three words, maximum efficiency. 'Party's over' captures George's deflationary worldview perfectly. Strong economy score.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. That suit is not for sale.

dialogue.speaker: Man
dialogue.line: What do you mean? It's on the rack.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I know, but I'm thinking about it. I'm very interested in that suit. I might buy it.

dialogue.speaker: Clerk
dialogue.line: Sir, that suit came in this morning. It's been on the rack for three hours.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: See? He's been staring at it all day. He's obsessed.

dialogue.speaker: Clerk
dialogue.line: Actually, you've been staring at it. You came in, looked at it, and we haven't seen you since.

jokes.text: The salesperson tells George about an 'unadvertised sale' starting Friday — the suit will be half-price.
jokes.setup: George is about to give up on the suit due to price.
jokes.punchline: The 'unadvertised sale' reveal gives George hope — but the very act of the salesperson advertising an unadvertised sale will become a running joke.
jokes.explanation: Setup for a running gag that pays off later. The 'unadvertised sale' concept plants the seed. Gains rewatch value.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: An unadvertised sale? That's a contradiction!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What do you mean? It's unadvertised.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: But you're advertising it right now by telling me about it!

jokes.text: Jerry interviews another restaurant customer who had pie offered to them — they gave a REASON for declining. Jerry: 'You didn't just shake your head. No. I'm not a psycho. Exactly. You're not a psycho.'
jokes.setup: Jerry has been so disturbed by Audrey's silent pie refusal that he's interviewing strangers in restaurants to establish a baseline.
jokes.punchline: The stranger's confirmation that giving a reason is normal behavior leads Jerry to officially diagnose Audrey as a 'psycho' through Socratic method.
jokes.explanation: This is a standout scene. The idea that Jerry is so bothered by the pie refusal that he's conducting field research in restaurants is perfectly absurd Seinfeld logic. 'I'm not a psycho. Exactly.' — the casual, matter-of-fact delivery of this damning verdict is excellent. Justifies 8+.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So I'm looking at this pie, right? And I'm thinking, where did this come from? Who made it? These are important questions.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I have exhibits. Exhibit A: the crust. Notice the golden-brown coloring. Very suspicious. Exhibit B: the filling. Consistent throughout. Too consistent.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And exhibit C, ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the tin. A standard tin. But whose tin? That is the real mystery.

jokes.text: Jerry: 'Well, I think we've proven who the psycho is.' — said smugly to himself after the interview.
jokes.setup: Jerry has just conducted his informal research.
jokes.punchline: His self-satisfied conclusion — completely disproportionate to the triviality of the pie situation — is the punchline.
jokes.explanation: Perfect button on the restaurant interview scene. Jerry's sense of scientific triumph over a trivial grievance is very much in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're not washing your hands?

dialogue.speaker: Poppie
dialogue.line: I'm making pizza!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: But you just came out of the bathroom!

dialogue.speaker: Poppie
dialogue.line: A little dirt, it's good for you. Builds up the immune system.

jokes.text: Kramer wants someone to scratch his back. Jerry: 'I think you know my policy.' Then: 'I'm going home to spatula.'
jokes.setup: Kramer asks Jerry to scratch his back. Jerry declines, citing 'my policy.'
jokes.punchline: 'I'm going home to spatula' — Jerry has a kitchen tool designated for back-scratching. The existence of 'the policy' and then 'the spatula' creates a full absurd domestic system.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy. 'I think you know my policy' establishes a whole unspoken social code. Then 'I'm going home to spatula' is one of the best lines in the episode — it implies Jerry has solved the back-scratching problem so thoroughly that a spatula is his dedicated tool. Maximum funny per word. Justifies 8+.

dialogue.speaker: Poppie
dialogue.line: You want pie? I got pie. Beautiful pie.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What kind of pie?

dialogue.speaker: Poppie
dialogue.line: Chocolate cream. My wife made it. Well, she didn't make it, but she bought it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't know, Poppie. After what happened last time...

dialogue.speaker: Poppie
dialogue.line: Last time? What last time? This pie is fresh. Perfectly good pie.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's what you said about the other pie.

jokes.text: George is 'guarding the suit' at the store. Jerry says this casually; Elaine says 'He's guarding a suit?'
jokes.setup: Jerry explains where George is.
jokes.punchline: Elaine's incredulous reaction to George physically guarding a retail suit captures the absurdity of what George is doing.
jokes.explanation: Good reaction beat. The concept of guarding a suit is inherently funny and Elaine's incredulity voices what the audience is thinking.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It was a wild scene.

jokes.text: Olive offers to scratch Kramer's back. Kramer: 'Madam, I pray you're not toying with me.'
jokes.setup: Kramer has been desperately seeking a back scratcher all episode.
jokes.punchline: His overwrought, formal plea — 'Madam, I pray you're not toying with me' — for something as simple as a back scratch.
jokes.explanation: Kramer's formal Victorian register applied to a back itch is perfect character comedy. 'Madam, I pray you're not toying with me' is an absurdly elevated response to a mundane offer. High character integration — only Kramer would phrase it this way. Justifies 8+.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You didn't wash your hands!

dialogue.speaker: Poppie
dialogue.line: I washed 'em.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You did not wash your hands. I was standing right there. You came out of the bathroom, and you went right to the pizza.

dialogue.speaker: Poppie
dialogue.line: So what? I make the pizza.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what?! So what?! You can't make pizza with your hands like that! At least go through the motions. Pretend you washed your hands. Pretend!

jokes.text: George is caught trying on the suit again at the store — the manager confronts him.
jokes.setup: George was told he couldn't have the suit held. He was supposed to be 'guarding' it.
jokes.punchline: He's been caught wearing it — he's gone from guarding it to trying it on in secret.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the suit subplot. George's inability to resist the suit even while theoretically guarding it is very in-character.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I pretend to wash my hands sometimes.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I do that all the time.

jokes.text: A random man in the store tries to buy the suit; George, who doesn't work there, intervenes to stop him by lying about the sale being on 'Monday' instead of Friday.
jokes.setup: Someone else wants to buy George's suit.
jokes.punchline: George pretends to be a helpful stranger while actually sabotaging the other customer with false sale information.
jokes.explanation: Classic George — self-serving deception dressed as helpfulness. The use of the 'unadvertised sale' information as a weapon is a smart callback.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So Poppie doesn't wash his hands. That's not poor hygiene, that's a cry for help.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: A cry for help? The man's a restaurateur. He's handling food.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Exactly. He wants someone to notice. 'Look at me, I'm dirty. Pay attention to me.'

jokes.text: The original salesperson corrects George: 'Actually, the unadvertised sale starts on Friday.' Customer says 'Friday? Thanks.' George is defeated.
jokes.setup: George lied about the sale day to keep the other customer away.
jokes.punchline: The salesperson casually blows George's cover by correcting the day — undermining his sabotage immediately.
jokes.explanation: Good structural payoff. George's plan is immediately undone by the very salesperson who told him about the sale in the first place.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: She has a very unusual technique. She uses her nails, her knuckles, sometimes the back of her hand. It's like a full-body experience.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's just a back scratch, Kramer.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No, no, no. You don't understand. She starts at the shoulders, works her way down real slow. She's got this rhythm, like she's playing an instrument.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: An instrument?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah! And then she gets to the lower back and she just... stays there. For a while. Really works it. It's sensual, Jerry. It's very sensual.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Kramer, it's a back scratch!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You're not listening to me! The woman is an artist!

jokes.text: George to the salesperson: 'You know, for an unadvertised sale, you're doing a lot of yapping about it.'
jokes.setup: The salesperson has just publicly advertised the 'unadvertised' sale for the second time.
jokes.punchline: George points out the fundamental contradiction — an unadvertised sale that everyone keeps announcing.
jokes.explanation: This is the best payoff of the running gag. 'A lot of yapping about it' is perfectly economical and captures the inherent absurdity of an 'unadvertised' sale that everyone keeps announcing. High quotability. Justifies 8+: it's a sharp observational point that also functions as a perfectly-timed character moment.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, I was looking at it. It's got a great shape. Very lifelike. I'm thinking of taking it home.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What? It's a mannequin. It's not like I'm doing anything wrong. It's for my apartment. I need something to talk to.

jokes.text: Jerry sees apple pie on the menu at Poppie's restaurant and his face falls — he can't help himself.
jokes.setup: Jerry is at dinner with Audrey at her father's restaurant, trying to drop the pie issue.
jokes.punchline: Apple pie is on the menu, reopening the wound — Jerry's face registers the torment.
jokes.explanation: Visual/reaction beat. Jerry's visible distress at seeing apple pie again is a good character moment that advances the running gag.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You hid my suit! You deliberately hid my suit!

dialogue.speaker: Man
dialogue.line: I didn't hide it, I just... misplaced it.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You misplaced it? In what, a locked safe? Behind a false wall?

jokes.text: Jerry asks Audrey if she's had the apple pie here. She confirms she's had it 'many times.'
jokes.setup: Jerry's obsession with the pie refusal is still unresolved.
jokes.punchline: Audrey has had apple pie many times — just not from Jerry's preferred restaurant. The mystery deepens absurdly.
jokes.explanation: Deepens the mystery nicely. She's not anti-pie; she's anti-that-specific-pie. The motivation becomes more intriguing.

jokes.text: Jerry presents his case against Audrey using itemized evidence: 'You obviously like pies. You carry doughnuts in your bag. You're not averse to pastry.'
jokes.setup: Jerry is making the case for why Audrey's refusal was inexplicable.
jokes.punchline: He's built a formal argument — a legal brief about pie consumption. 'Not averse to pastry' as legal language for a dating grievance.
jokes.explanation: Jerry delivering a formal pastry brief to his girlfriend is exactly the kind of obsessive-over-nothing behavior that defines the show. 'Not averse to pastry' is particularly good.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He felt my face. With his hands. He was trying to see what I looked like.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: It was like... like he was a sculptor and my face was clay.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: His hands were all over my face. My cheeks, my nose, my forehead.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: And then he got to my lips and I thought, 'Okay, this is getting a little weird.'

jokes.text: Poppie emerges from the bathroom and begins kneading bread dough without washing his hands. Jerry watches in horror.
jokes.setup: Poppie has gone to the bathroom. Jerry is waiting for dinner.
jokes.punchline: Poppie comes out of the bathroom and immediately starts kneading dough — the implication of what's in the bread is the horror-comedy.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best structural moments. The scene is entirely visual — no dialogue needed (the transcript shows Jerry going silent). The delayed reveal of WHY Audrey wouldn't eat the pie from Monk's (her father, who bakes their pies, doesn't wash his hands) is perfectly earned. This moment restructures the entire episode retrospectively. Justifies 8.5+.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He didn't say hello to me in the coffee shop!

jokes.text: Audrey: 'Jerry, have some.' / 'You're not gonna taste it?' — mirroring his earlier relentless pie-pushing, but now from her perspective.
jokes.setup: Earlier Jerry badgered Audrey to taste the pie. Now he refuses to eat Poppie's cooking.
jokes.punchline: Audrey uses Jerry's exact logic against him — she's now the pie-pusher and he's the silent refuser.
jokes.explanation: This is the structural apex of the episode. The roles are perfectly reversed — Jerry is now Audrey, unable to explain why he won't taste the food, and Audrey is now Jerry, mystified by the refusal. The callback is built into the episode's architecture. The symmetry is impeccable. This is what great sitcom construction looks like. Justifies 8.8+.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's the fabric. It's a cotton blend.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Cotton blend? What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: When I walk, my thighs rub together and it makes that noise.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Your thighs rub together?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: The fabric is too smooth. It's like two pieces of silk.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what, you need a rougher fabric?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I need a natural weave. Something with texture. Corduroy, denim...

jokes.text: Jerry, are you okay? / Is anything wrong? / No. Nothing. You look like you've seen a ghost.
jokes.setup: Jerry has just witnessed Poppie's bathroom-to-dough maneuver
jokes.punchline: 'No. Nothing.' — Jerry's flat denial while visibly traumatized, and Audrey's 'you look like you've seen a ghost' which is more accurate than she knows
jokes.explanation: A solid dramatic irony beat. 'You look like you've seen a ghost' is an unwitting understatement — what he's seen is arguably worse. Jerry's 'No. Nothing' while clearly disturbed is a good performance-dependent joke.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: She fired someone for involuntary nose whistling.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Involuntary nose whistling?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, the guy couldn't help it. It just came out.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's not a fireable offense!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Tell that to MacKenzie.

jokes.text: Jerry explains he couldn't tell Audrey why he didn't eat because 'It's not the kind of thing you wanna hear about your father.'
jokes.setup: Audrey thinks Jerry didn't eat the food to get back at her for the pie refusal.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's understatement — 'not the kind of thing you wanna hear' about watching your father knead dough with bathroom hands.
jokes.explanation: Good understatement. The restraint of 'not the kind of thing' to describe witnessing food contamination is appropriate and funny.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, I'm sitting in the waiting room, and I'm thinking about all the things that could go wrong in this interview.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What if I say something stupid? What if my hair looks bad? What if there's a draft in the room and I'm whooshing all over the place?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I mean, how am I supposed to concentrate on selling myself if I'm whooshing?

jokes.text: George: 'How could he not have washed? Even if you're not gonna soap up, at least pretend, for my benefit. Turn the water on. Do something.'
jokes.setup: The group is discussing Poppie's bathroom habits.
jokes.punchline: George's escalating demand — not even asking for actual hand-washing, just the PERFORMANCE of hand-washing — 'Turn the water on. Do something.'
jokes.explanation: Excellent George speech. The lowered bar — from 'wash your hands' to 'at least pretend' to 'just turn the water on' — is perfectly calibrated escalation. 'Do something' as a final plea is the right button. High character integration: George's pragmatic moral compromise is very him. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Jerry: 'Yeah, just like I do.' — casually admitting he also just runs the water without washing.
jokes.setup: George has just said he'd accept the minimum standard of just turning on the water.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's straight-faced confession that he does the same thing — a betrayal of the very outrage they've been expressing.
jokes.explanation: Three words that detonate the entire moral high ground the characters have been standing on. Jerry's admission undercuts all the outrage and is perfectly timed. Maximum economy — four syllables do enormous comedy work. One of the episode's best lines. Justifies 8.4+.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's my butt!

jokes.text: Kramer: 'You know, a chef who doesn't wash is like a cop who steals. It's a cry for help. He wants to get caught.'
jokes.setup: The group is discussing Poppie's hygiene failure.
jokes.punchline: Kramer's pop-psychology take — framing Poppie's hand-not-washing as a subconscious cry for help, using a bizarre law enforcement analogy.
jokes.explanation: Outstanding Kramer line. The analogy between a dirty-handed chef and a thieving cop is not just absurd — it follows its own internal logic completely. 'He wants to get caught' applies armchair psychology with total confidence to an utterly mundane hygiene lapse. Only Kramer delivers psychiatric diagnoses with this certainty. Justifies 8.5+: it's original, structurally sound, and completely character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Your Honor, I'd like to cite the precedent set in Dummy v. State, 1987. The court ruled that a ventriloquist dummy has certain inalienable rights.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Counselor, that's not a real case.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Not yet, Your Honor. But I'm prepared to argue it here today.

jokes.text: Jerry: 'I think Poppie's got some problems. There's a whole other thing going on with Poppie.'
jokes.setup: Kramer has just psychoanalyzed Poppie.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's vague, understated diagnosis — 'a whole other thing going on' — suggests depths to Poppie's issues that we can only imagine.
jokes.explanation: Good button line. 'There's a whole other thing going on with Poppie' as a vague, ominous summary of a man's hygiene sins is nicely understated.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What is that? A mannequin? Why would they put a mannequin in the car?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's supposed to make you want to buy the car. Two for the price of one.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Like the Doublemint twins?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Exactly! Double your pleasure, double your fun.

jokes.text: Kramer describes Olive's back-scratching technique: 'the crisscross, the figure eight, strumming the old banjo, and this wild, savage free-for-all where anything can happen.'
jokes.setup: Kramer is rhapsodizing about Olive's back-scratching abilities.
jokes.punchline: 'Strumming the old banjo' and 'wild, savage free-for-all where anything can happen' — the erotic escalation of back-scratch terminology.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional Kramer monologue. The technical vocabulary applied to back-scratching ('figure eight,' 'crisscross') escalates into the sexually suggestive 'strumming the old banjo' and the breathlessly described 'wild, savage free-for-all where anything can happen.' Only Kramer would describe this as a kind of transcendent experience. High quotability. Justifies 8.4+.

jokes.text: George: 'Yeah. And Poppie's got problems.' — casual transition connecting two unrelated absurdities.
jokes.setup: The conversation has moved through the naked mannequin and Poppie's issues.
jokes.punchline: George sums everything up in one perfectly dry line that treats both absurdities as equivalent facts of life.
jokes.explanation: A throwaway line that's better than it has any right to be. The flat delivery treating 'naked mannequin' and 'Poppie's psychological problems' as items of equal weight is very George. Strong economy.

dialogue.speaker: Board of Health Official
dialogue.line: We've received complaints about serious health code violations at your restaurant.

dialogue.speaker: Poppie
dialogue.line: Violations? What violations? My restaurant is spotless!

dialogue.speaker: Board of Health Official
dialogue.line: We found rodent droppings, expired food, and a cockroach infestation.

dialogue.speaker: Poppie
dialogue.line: So we got a few bugs. Big deal.

jokes.text: George discovers the suit is missing from where he left it — then finds it in the back 'misplaced,' and the salesperson claims ignorance. George: 'You hid the suit!'
jokes.setup: George has been obsessing over the suit and guarding it.
jokes.punchline: The salesperson has hidden the suit to prevent George from having it — the conflict reaches its peak.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the suit subplot. The salesperson denying the obvious ('I have no idea how this suit got misplaced') is a satisfying villain beat.

jokes.text: The salesperson: 'Nevertheless, I do believe I shall purchase it.' George: 'I hope you rot in that suit.'
jokes.setup: The salesperson has outmaneuvered George for the suit.
jokes.punchline: 'I hope you rot in that suit' — George's curse is specific to the item of conflict, delightfully petty.
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable George line. The specificity of 'in that suit' turns a standard curse into a tailored (pun intended) George grievance. The pettiness is perfectly calibrated.

dialogue.speaker: MacKenzie
dialogue.line: Here, try this pie.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, I'm not hungry.

dialogue.speaker: MacKenzie
dialogue.line: Just try it.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I don't want any pie.

dialogue.speaker: MacKenzie
dialogue.line: Eat the pie, George.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Why are you pushing pie on me?

dialogue.speaker: MacKenzie
dialogue.line: EAT THE PIE!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, I'll eat your pie!

jokes.text: George: 'I'll get you for this. I don't know how, but I'm gonna get you. You are gonna pay.' Salesperson: 'Oh, I'll pay... Half-price. Arrivederci, my fellow 40-short.'
jokes.setup: George threatens vague retribution.
jokes.punchline: 'Oh, I'll pay... Half-price.' The salesperson twists George's threat into a celebratory reference to the sale price, then dismisses him with 'my fellow 40-short' — acknowledging they share a small body type while humiliating him.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's very best jokes. The pivot from George's impotent threat to 'Oh, I'll pay... Half-price' is structurally perfect — it subverts 'pay' in a completely unexpected direction. 'Arrivederci, my fellow 40-short' is a masterful parting shot: it dismisses George while acknowledging their physical similarity, making the victory even more cutting. Justifies 8.6+.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I didn't get the job.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: But I found out why — the pie was contaminated.

jokes.text: Elaine explains her list of suspects for the mannequin's origin: 'There's a blind guy at a party I was at who felt my face for a really long time... He almost put his finger up my nose.'
jokes.setup: Elaine is trying to figure out who made a mannequin in her likeness.
jokes.punchline: Her first suspect is a blind man who felt her face — the detail 'almost put his finger up my nose' is a specific, gross, and wonderful touch.
jokes.explanation: Elaine's absurd detective work. The blind-man-as-sculptor theory is imaginative, and the nostril detail makes it specific enough to be vivid and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I had this itch on my back, I couldn't reach it. So I asked this guy to scratch it for me. He scratched it, but it wasn't the right spot. So I'm trying to direct him - 'No, up a little, to the left, down, back up.' And I realize I'm being too hard on the guy. So I just tell him, 'That's it, that's the spot!' Even though it wasn't.

jokes.text: Jerry says 'I won't tell you the rest of the list' because he didn't think the blind guy did it. Elaine: 'Because you have an attitude.'
jokes.setup: Jerry dismisses Elaine's suspect list.
jokes.punchline: Elaine's accusation that Jerry has 'an attitude' — said with complete conviction about a list of suspects for a mannequin crime.
jokes.explanation: Mild but functional exchange. Elaine's wounded dignity about her mannequin investigation is in character.

dialogue.speaker: Olive
dialogue.line: Why isn't the mannequin wearing any clothes?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Underwear is a style.

jokes.text: George walks in wearing the new suit, clearly thrilled. The gang reacts to a strange sound — a 'swoosh' when he walks.
jokes.setup: George has apparently obtained the suit by some means.
jokes.punchline: The suit makes a loud swooshing sound when he walks — an absurd physical defect that will torpedo his job interview.
jokes.explanation: The swooshing suit is a well-executed visual/physical gag that pays off the entire suit subplot with an absurd defect. The escalating realization of why the noise is a catastrophe for the interview is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait, there's more than one?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, I found three of them!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Three Elaines? That's either the best thing that ever happened or the worst.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I'm thinking best.

jokes.text: 'It's rubbing between your thighs when you walk. That's what's making that swooshy sound.'
jokes.setup: The swooshing sound from George's suit
jokes.punchline: Jerry's clinical explanation — 'rubbing between your thighs' — as the cause of the swoosh is both an accurate description and a body-image dig
jokes.explanation: Jerry's matter-of-fact diagnosis of the thigh-rub problem delivers the joke cleanly. The implication (George's thighs are large enough to make noise) is understated but present. Functional supporting joke to the visual gag.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So the mannequin creator named his creation after Elaine?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's right. He saw her on the street and was so inspired, he decided to name the whole line after her.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's insane. Elaine doesn't even know this guy.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: This is the kind of thing that happens in real life and nobody believes you.

jokes.text: Jerry identifies the source of the sound: 'It's rubbing between your thighs when you walk.'
jokes.setup: Everyone is puzzled by the mysterious swooshing sound.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's matter-of-fact diagnosis — the fabric rubbing between George's thighs — is delivered with clinical detachment.
jokes.explanation: Good beat. The thigh-rubbing detail is both physically accurate and slightly humiliating for George, which is perfect.

jokes.text: George: 'I didn't hear it on the way over because of the street noise.'
jokes.setup: George is absorbing the revelation about the swooshing.
jokes.punchline: His immediate realization that the city noise was masking his suit defect — the panic of all the silent indoor scenarios where he'll be exposed.
jokes.explanation: A small but perfectly George moment. The detail about street noise is an astute comic observation about urban life, and George's dawning horror at what this means is excellent character work.

jokes.text: George: 'This is no good! I gotta meet these guys from MacKenzie for lunch!'
jokes.setup: The swooshing suit problem has just been identified.
jokes.punchline: George's immediate catastrophizing — the suit was meant to seal the interview, and now it will doom it.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation moment. George's disaster thinking is in full gear.

jokes.text: George explains MacKenzie fired the last guy because 'his nose whistled when he breathed.' George: 'So you're not gonna get the job because your pants make noise?'
jokes.setup: Jerry asks why MacKenzie would care about a noisy suit.
jokes.punchline: The precedent is already established — MacKenzie fired someone for a whistling nose. A whooshing suit is on the same spectrum.
jokes.explanation: The whistling nose detail is inspired — it creates a credible world where involuntary body/clothing noises are legitimate grounds for termination. 'So you're not gonna get the job because your pants make noise?' is a perfectly calibrated absurdist premise stated flatly. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: George: 'Let's say it's between me and one other guy. He's got a quiet suit, and I'm whooshing all over the place. Who do you think he's gonna hire?'
jokes.setup: George is making the case for why the noisy suit is a professional disqualifier.
jokes.punchline: 'I'm whooshing all over the place' — George's perfectly specific term for the suit noise, delivered as the decisive factor in an employment decision.
jokes.explanation: George's paranoid professional logic reaches full expression here. 'Whooshing all over the place' is a great phrase. The suit-as-disqualifier argument, while absurd, is delivered with complete conviction.

jokes.text: The waiter at Monk's tells Jerry that Audrey came in and had a piece of apple pie.
jokes.setup: Jerry has been unable to figure out why Audrey wouldn't eat the pie from Monk's.
jokes.punchline: Audrey had the apple pie at Monk's without Jerry — meaning she was fine with it all along and only refused while with him.
jokes.explanation: This revelation deepens the mystery — Audrey isn't afraid of apple pie, she specifically wouldn't eat it WITH Jerry. The running gag takes an unexpected turn. Jerry's reaction 'This woman is bending my mind into a pretzel' is the right button.

jokes.text: Jerry: 'This woman is bending my mind into a pretzel.'
jokes.setup: Jerry learns Audrey ate the pie without him.
jokes.punchline: The pretzel metaphor for cognitive distress over a trivial mystery is perfectly Jerry — over-dramatic about the unimportant.
jokes.explanation: Good Jerry line. 'Bending my mind into a pretzel' over a pie is appropriately hyperbolic.

jokes.text: The lingerie saleswoman recognizes Elaine as the mannequin: 'You were wearing a G-string and one of those bras with points.'
jokes.setup: Elaine has been trying to track down who made the mannequin.
jokes.punchline: A random woman recognizes Elaine as the mannequin and addresses her by the mannequin's outfit — treating the plastic dummy as the real Elaine.
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment. The specificity of 'G-string and one of those bras with points' makes this vivid. The woman conflating Elaine with the mannequin's outfit is appropriately awkward.

jokes.text: Elaine to the store owner: 'That's my ass in your window.'
jokes.setup: Elaine has confirmed the mannequin is in the store window in a sexualized pose.
jokes.punchline: 'That's my ass in your window' — Elaine's direct, territorial claim over her plastic doppelganger's posterior.
jokes.explanation: One of the most quotable lines in the episode. Six words that perfectly capture Elaine's indignation, the absurdity of the situation, and her complete conviction. The bluntness of 'my ass' in contrast with the formal commercial context is perfectly calibrated. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: Store owner: 'It's our mannequin. We can do whatever we want.'
jokes.setup: Elaine has demanded the mannequin be taken down.
jokes.punchline: The owner's corporate property-rights response to a personal dignity complaint is a perfect bureaucratic non-sequitur.
jokes.explanation: Good straight-man line that sets up the escalation. The gap between the legal property argument and Elaine's dignity complaint is the comedy.

jokes.text: Kramer claims to be Elaine's attorney and cites 'Winchell v. Mahoney. The Charlie McCarthy hearings.' as legal precedent.
jokes.setup: Elaine is threatening legal action over the mannequin.
jokes.punchline: Kramer invokes 'The Charlie McCarthy hearings' as legal precedent — Charlie McCarthy being a ventriloquist's dummy, making it the perfect absurd precedent for a mannequin case.
jokes.explanation: Outstanding Kramer moment. 'The Charlie McCarthy hearings' is a perfect piece of invented legal history — Charlie McCarthy was a famous ventriloquist dummy, making it the ideal case to cite for mannequin rights. The specificity of 'Winchell v. Mahoney' (Edgar Bergen's real name was Bergen; Winchell was another ventriloquist) adds a layer that rewards knowledgeable viewers. Kramer's total confidence as fake legal counsel is perfect. Justifies 8.8+.

jokes.text: Kramer announces 'Jerry, get the car' while Jerry protests as 'legal counsel.' Kramer: 'As your legal counsel, I must advise against this.'
jokes.setup: Elaine is about to do something drastic with the mannequin.
jokes.punchline: Jerry protests as Kramer's self-appointed 'legal counsel' — the role reversal of Kramer in charge and Jerry as the cautious lawyer is absurd.
jokes.explanation: Good extension of the attorney bit. Jerry as cautious legal counsel while Kramer acts recklessly is a funny inversion.

jokes.text: After the mannequin incident, Jerry says: 'I don't know about you, but I'm getting a hankering for some Doublemint gum.'
jokes.setup: Elaine has just done something with the mannequin (presumably taken it).
jokes.punchline: Doublemint gum uses identical twins as its mascot — Jerry is referencing the mannequin/Elaine duality with an ad reference.
jokes.explanation: Sharp, oblique pop-culture callback. The Doublemint twins connection to the Elaine-mannequin situation requires a second's thought, which makes the payoff more satisfying. Very economical and witty. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: George is at the MacKenzie lunch interview. His suit starts making the whooshing sound again. MacKenzie and colleagues exchange looks.
jokes.setup: George needs to impress MacKenzie at this lunch for the job.
jokes.punchline: The suit betrays him — the whooshing sound erupts in the quiet professional environment.
jokes.explanation: Good payoff of the suit gag. The professional stakes make the absurd noise funnier — the gap between George's professional aspirations and his whooshing pants is the joke.

jokes.text: MacKenzie: 'It's quieter down here. We can concentrate without people whooshing around.'
jokes.setup: They've moved to a quieter area, ostensibly to concentrate.
jokes.punchline: MacKenzie unwittingly describes George as the whooshing person — 'without people whooshing around' — which is exactly what George's suit does.
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural irony. MacKenzie's completely oblivious use of the word 'whooshing' — the exact word George used to describe his suit problem — is a perfectly timed ironic callback. George must endure hearing his nemesis described by the very man he's trying to impress. Justifies 8.2+.

jokes.text: MacKenzie hears the sound again. George denies hearing anything. MacKenzie: 'Kind of like a rustling. Could be the leaves.'
jokes.setup: George is desperately pretending the noise doesn't exist.
jokes.punchline: MacKenzie helpfully suggests 'could be the leaves' — they're indoors, making his explanation absurd, but also giving George a lifeline he can't fully use.
jokes.explanation: The cringe escalates nicely. 'Could be the leaves' indoors is a good absurd detail.

jokes.text: 'Board of Health. We've had complaints.' / 'What's the problem?' / 'What do they want from Poppie?' / 'Well, Poppie's a little sloppy.'
jokes.setup: Jerry has called the Board of Health on Poppie's restaurant
jokes.punchline: Jerry's understatement 'Poppie's a little sloppy' — for a chef who kneads dough after using the bathroom without washing
jokes.explanation: 'Poppie's a little sloppy' is a great line — a masterpiece of understatement for what is a serious food hygiene violation. The euphemistic 'sloppy' for what we know he did is darkly funny and perfectly economical.

jokes.text: MacKenzie reveals the last employee was fired not for a whistling nose but for being 'not a team player.' MacKenzie: 'That's something we don't joke about at MacKenzie.'
jokes.setup: George has been terrified his noisy suit would get him fired like the last guy with the whistling nose.
jokes.punchline: The whistling-nose story was wrong — the guy was fired for a completely mundane corporate reason, undercutting George's paranoia.
jokes.explanation: Smart misdirection. The entire suit paranoia was based on a false premise. 'That's something we don't joke about at MacKenzie' as an ominous corporate warning about dissent adds a whole new absurd dimension.

jokes.text: George: 'No problem there. Conformity's an obsession with me.'
jokes.setup: MacKenzie has declared they don't tolerate dissent.
jokes.punchline: 'Conformity's an obsession with me' — George presenting his natural tendency toward compliance and people-pleasing as a professional virtue.
jokes.explanation: One of George's best lines in the episode. 'Conformity's an obsession with me' perfectly captures his entire character — his desperate need for approval, his spinelessness, and his attempt to market his worst qualities as assets. Five words that define the character. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: The waiter brings 'chocolate cream pie, compliments of the house. The chef made it special for you.'
jokes.setup: The lunch is going well; George might get the job.
jokes.punchline: The pie is from Poppie's restaurant — establishing the dark setup for the mass food poisoning payoff.
jokes.explanation: The structural setup for the pie poisoning payoff. 'The chef made it special for you' takes on a horrifying meaning in context.

jokes.text: MacKenzie insists George take a bite of pie: 'Take a bite, George. It's delicious. I insist. If you're one of us, you'll take a bite.'
jokes.setup: George is about to refuse the pie (which comes from Poppie's, whose hygiene is suspect).
jokes.punchline: MacKenzie's 'if you're one of us, you'll take a bite' frames pie-eating as a loyalty test — directly mirroring the episode's central pie-refusal theme but with reversed power dynamics.
jokes.explanation: The episode's thematic and structural apex. 'If you're one of us, you'll take a bite' is the pie theme weaponized — pie as social contract, as loyalty test. George is now the one who can't explain why he won't eat, mirroring Audrey, mirroring Jerry with Poppie's food. All three storylines converge in this one moment. The callbacks are so layered that this scene rewards every prior setup. Justifies 8.9+.

jokes.text: George: 'So you didn't get the job?' Jerry: 'No. But I was the only one at the table that didn't get violently ill.'
jokes.setup: The MacKenzie lunch has concluded off-screen.
jokes.punchline: Everyone who ate Poppie's pie got food poisoning — George's refusal to eat, despite losing the job, saved him from violent illness. The consolation prize is avoiding a medical emergency.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Seinfeld denouement. 'I was the only one at the table that didn't get violently ill' as George's silver lining is one of the best lines in the episode — the deadpan delivery of a non-victory as the best outcome. Maximum economy (one sentence to resolve two major subplots). Justifies 8.7+.

jokes.text: Kramer tells Elaine he's been faking the back itch for two days so as not to hurt Olive's feelings.
jokes.setup: The back itch subplot has been resolved; Kramer now has to end things with Olive.
jokes.punchline: Kramer has been FAKING an itch for days out of consideration — an absurd act of kindness that's also completely insane.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully Kramer. He's simultaneously thoughtful (not wanting to hurt Olive) and completely deranged (pretending to itch for two days). The inversion of the usual Kramer selfishness makes it surprising.

jokes.text: Kramer breaks up with Olive by pointing to the Elaine mannequin — 'There she is. That's my gal.' Olive: 'You're a liar. I've seen her before. She's not your girlfriend.'
jokes.setup: Kramer needs to break up with Olive and has chosen the mannequin as his 'other woman.'
jokes.punchline: He's using the Elaine mannequin as his fictional new girlfriend — and Olive immediately calls him on it.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant convergence of the mannequin subplot and the Olive subplot. Using the Elaine mannequin as a fake girlfriend to end a back-scratching arrangement is maximally absurd. The fact that Olive immediately doesn't buy it makes it funnier. Justifies 8.4+.

jokes.text: Olive: 'Why is she wearing her underwear?' Kramer: 'Well, it's a style.'
jokes.setup: The mannequin (still undressed/in underwear from when it was removed from the store) is being used as Kramer's 'girlfriend.'
jokes.punchline: Kramer's completely breezy justification — 'it's a style' — for his plastic girlfriend being in underwear.
jokes.explanation: Three words of maximum economy. 'It's a style' as the totality of Kramer's defense for his underwear-clad mannequin girlfriend is perfect. The confidence and casualness is pure Kramer. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Why is she wearing her underwear? / Well, it's a style.
jokes.setup: Olive has been introduced to the Elaine mannequin as Kramer's 'girlfriend'; it's in lingerie
jokes.punchline: Olive asks why 'she' is in underwear; Kramer explains it as a fashion choice
jokes.explanation: Kramer explaining the stolen mannequin's underwear as 'a style' — maintaining the fiction of a mannequin girlfriend with complete seriousness — is Kramer at his most committed. The gap between the absurdity and his straight delivery is the joke engine.

jokes.text: Jerry: 'You ever solve the riddle of the pie?' — summing up the episode's central mystery casually.
jokes.setup: The episode is wrapping up.
jokes.punchline: 'One for the ages' — the pie mystery will never be officially resolved, and Jerry characterizes it as a profound, eternal riddle.
jokes.explanation: 'One for the ages' is a perfect Seinfeld button — the non-resolution of the trivial mystery elevated to philosophical status.

jokes.text: Jerry: 'But I think they're gonna put Poppie away for a long, long time.'
jokes.setup: The Poppie hygiene subplot is being closed out.
jokes.punchline: The casual prediction that Poppie will be imprisoned ('put away for a long, long time') for restaurant hygiene violations — as if it's a serious crime.
jokes.explanation: Good dark button line. The severity of 'put away for a long, long time' for not washing hands is a funny escalation.

jokes.text: Elaine learns from a friend in Chicago that there's another mannequin there that looks like her — 'What if there are more? Where are they coming from?'
jokes.setup: The mannequin mystery seems solved after the store mannequin was removed.
jokes.punchline: The horror-movie reveal — there are MORE Elaine mannequins, and their origin is unknown. The mundane story becomes a conspiracy thriller.
jokes.explanation: Excellent closing escalation. 'Where are they coming from?' frames the mannequin story as an existential horror, which is a great tonal pivot for a tag scene. The Body Snatchers callback circles back perfectly.

jokes.text: Cut to the mannequin designer telling his assistant about the 'tremendous response' to 'the TR-6 mannequin.' He corrects himself: 'I prefer to think of her as... Elaine.'
jokes.setup: The episode has ended with Elaine's horror at multiplying mannequins.
jokes.punchline: The final scene reveals a mysterious designer who has named the mannequin series after Elaine — a final, perfect dark tag that explains everything and nothing.
jokes.explanation: The episode's final and best structural payoff. The reveal of the designer who calls the mannequin 'Elaine' provides a satisfying but deeply unsettling resolution — the mystery is solved but the implications are creepier than the problem. The cold pivot from corporate terminology ('TR-6') to the intimate 'I prefer to think of her as... Elaine' is perfectly paced. Justifies 8.7+.

tmdb_overview: A mannequin resembles Elaine; George battles for a bargain suit; Jerry wonders why his date won't try a pie; Kramer has an itch.
insight: Observational humor drives a solid mid-tier entry with 56 jokes across 23 minutes.
